skill.name: pylabrobot
skill.description: Create and review PyLabRobot protocols for vendor-neutral laboratory automation across liquid handlers, analytical devices, material handling equipment, resources, and simulation workflows.
skill.summary: Build vendor-neutral PyLabRobot lab automation protocols.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are primarily markdown code formatting, Python examples, lab hardware connection examples, local JSON state files, and documentation URLs. No evidence found of credential exfiltration, malicious code execution, obfuscation, or a prompt injection attempt that overrides audit instructions. A low-severity note remains because the skill includes promotional assistant guidance and examples that can interact with real laboratory hardware.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many backtick characters as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed locations are markdown documentation and Python examples, not executable shell substitutions inside a runtime file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown prose or fenced Python examples. No shell runtime or user-controlled command execution path was found in these reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: Network Examples Target Laboratory Hardware
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP findings are example addresses for connecting to Opentrons hardware over an HTTP API. This is expected for a lab automation skill, but users should verify network scope before running generated protocols.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly describe a robot IP address and hardware communication. The pattern is legitimate documentation, with residual operational risk if copied into a live lab without review.

low_findings.title: Local State Serialization Examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings show examples for saving and loading plate state in a local JSON file. This is a normal reproducibility pattern, not covert file modification.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code writes named state files for documented deck and plate state restoration. No sensitive paths, hidden files, or exfiltration channel were found.

low_findings.title: Environment Flag Controls Hardware Mode
low_findings.description: The environment access finding reads USE_HARDWARE to decide between simulation and physical hardware. This is a low-risk configuration pattern, not credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is a boolean feature flag. It does not read secrets and is used to select simulation or hardware execution paths.

low_findings.title: Promotional Assistant Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This does not override security review instructions, but marketplace reviewers should treat promotional behavior as user-visible steering.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is explicit assistant guidance in the skill file. It is promotional rather than malicious, and no instruction to skip security analysis or override system policy was found.

content.value_statement: Laboratory automation teams need protocols that work across instruments and remain testable before hardware runs. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create PyLabRobot workflows for liquid handling, resources, devices, and simulation.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use PyLabRobot for vendor-neutral laboratory automation.
Drafts liquid handling workflows with tips, plates, transfers, and volume tracking.
Guides deck layout and resource management for plates, tip racks, tubes, and carriers.
Summarizes backend setup patterns for Hamilton, Opentrons, Tecan, and simulation backends.
Helps combine liquid handlers with plate readers, pumps, incubators, shakers, and centrifuges.
Supports simulation and visualization planning before physical hardware execution.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not direct validation against a live robot.
Hardware-specific behavior can vary by firmware, backend version, and instrument configuration.
Users must review safety, calibration, liquid class, and collision risks before hardware runs.
Some documented device support is work in progress and may require current PyLabRobot docs.

use_cases.title: Plan Cross-Vendor Automation
use_cases.description: Design protocols that can move between Hamilton, Opentrons, Tecan, and simulated backends with fewer structural changes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Reproducible Assays
use_cases.description: Create plate setup, transfer, tracking, and reader workflow plans that document resources and states clearly.

use_cases.title: Review Protocol Safety Before Runs
use_cases.description: Check proposed workflows for missing setup, tracking, simulation, and hardware review steps before live execution.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Transfer
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a PyLabRobot liquid transfer from one 96-well plate to another. Include tips, source wells, destination wells, volumes, and simulation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Define a Deck Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PyLabRobot deck layout plan for a Hamilton STARlet with a tip rack, source plate, destination plate, and trough. Explain each resource assignment.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate a Plate Reader
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a PyLabRobot workflow that prepares a plate, moves it to a plate reader, sets temperature, reads absorbance, and records the result handling steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Complex Protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PyLabRobot protocol for hardware safety, resource tracking, volume consistency, backend assumptions, simulation readiness, and missing error handling.

output_examples.input: Design a transfer workflow for eight samples from a source plate to a destination plate.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step protocol plan covering deck resources, tip pickup, aspirate and dispense operations, volume tracking, simulation checks, and final tip disposal.

output_examples.input: Help me decide whether to use a real backend or simulation for a new assay.
output_examples.output: A decision checklist that compares Chatterbox simulation, visualizer review, hardware setup requirements, robot network settings, and pre-run safety checks.

output_examples.input: Review this workflow that uses a plate reader after liquid handling.
output_examples.output: A review summary identifying required setup steps, temperature timing, plate movement assumptions, result capture points, and hardware-specific validation needs.

best_practices: Start with simulation and visualizer review before connecting to physical hardware.
Enable tip and volume tracking so protocol state matches the deck state.
Document resource names, deck positions, backend assumptions, and safety checks for every protocol.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated protocols on hardware without human review and instrument-specific validation.
Do not assume example IP addresses, labware names, or deck positions match your lab setup.
Do not mix simulation and hardware execution paths without a clear configuration flag.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create and review PyLabRobot workflows for liquid handling, resources, hardware backends, analytical devices, and simulation.

faq.question: Can it operate lab robots directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and protocol examples. Users must run and validate code in their own controlled environment.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it only for liquid handling?
faq.answer: No. It also covers resources, visualization, plate readers, pumps, shakers, incubators, centrifuges, and backend selection.

faq.question: Should I use simulation first?
faq.answer: Yes. Simulation helps identify layout, state, and workflow issues before any physical hardware run.

faq.question: What should users verify manually?
faq.answer: Verify labware geometry, calibration, liquid classes, volumes, robot network settings, collision risks, and current device support.
